summary: This interview with a New York City high school principal explores the implementation challenges of Common Core math standards, focusing on how teacher evaluations, curriculum gaps, and excessive testing create a 'terrorizing regime' for educators. The principal explains that while CC standards exist, there is no CC-aligned curriculum in NY high schools, yet teachers are being evaluated on CC-aligned tests their students were never prepared for. The rollout created massive anxiety because teacher evaluations became tied to test scores before curriculum materials were available. Despite these problems, the principal argues testing shouldn't disappear entirely but needs to be 'dialed down' and made reasonable.
key_points: Teachers spend one-eighth of instructional time administering tests, plus additional time grading them, representing a massive loss of teaching time
The CC rollout created a fundamental sequencing problem: tests were deployed before curriculum existed, and raised expectations without addressing where students actually were
Standards, curriculum, and testing form an interconnected triangle - you cannot evaluate one without the others, yet they were rolled out in isolation
When 75% of students score 1s and 2s on a 4-point scale, mass failure becomes meaningless and tests simply get curved, undermining the entire accountability framework
Teacher evaluation tied to test scores before curriculum alignment gave principals data that could be weaponized against teachers they wanted to remove
money_quotes: The real problem is this: we've created these new standards to make things more difficult and more challenging without recognizing where kids are in the present moment.
What does failure mean when everyone fails? It just means the test was too hard, or that what the kids were being taught was not relevant to the test.
They rolled out the test before any CC curriculum.
Education is a bi-polar pendulum and it never stops in the middle.
There's no test that can't be gamed that's also easy to grade.
You could give everyone a bad number and then fire the people you want, right? Correct. Is that the goal? Under Bloomberg it was.
If teacher evaluations were taken out of the mix, we'd have a very different roll-out environment.
key_insight: The Common Core rollout became a 'terrorizing regime' for teachers not because of the standards themselves, but because high-stakes evaluations were tied to tests before any aligned curriculum existed, creating an impossible accountability trap.
